summary: Jeff Atwood compares Windows Media Center Edition 2005 with TiVo Series 2, finding MCE technically capable but frustratingly inferior in user interface design. While MCE 2005 represents Microsoft's mythical 'third version' with solid incremental improvements like DVD burning and multiple tuner support, its 10-foot interface falls short of TiVo's elegance. Atwood criticizes two specific UI failures: the painful telephone keypad text entry versus TiVo's intuitive letter grid, and the unnecessary Back button versus TiVo's simple left-right navigation paradigm. Despite calling MCE a 'fantastic product,' he questions why Microsoft reinvented proven UI patterns poorly.
key_points: Hardware MPEG-2 encoding cards are essential even for fast CPUs when recording video, with Hauppauge being the recommended brand despite higher cost
MCE 2005 as Microsoft's 'third version' delivers solid incremental improvements including built-in burning, better movie browsing, and multi-tuner support
TiVo's text entry interface using a visual letter grid is vastly superior to MCE's telephone keypad approach
TiVo's navigation paradigm of left-for-back and right-for-forward creates intuitive spatial associations that MCE ignores
Good UI patterns already exist and should be copied rather than reinvented poorly
money_quotes: Any time you're cribbing UI from a telephone, you are in deep, deep trouble.
Cursoring to the letter you want is incredibly intuitive. Obvious even. Everything that numeric keypad entry is not.
Even the fastest 3.4ghz+ CPUs struggle with real time video encoding – at least, if you want to have any hope of multitasking without dropping massive numbers of frames in your recordings.
It's just hard to stomach these obvious lost opportunities for copying UI elements that are so stunningly effective on the Tivo.
I've used the cheaper brands (I'm looking at you, Avermedia) and it isn't worth it.
key_insight: When designing user interfaces, companies should study and adopt proven patterns that work rather than reinventing inferior solutions out of pride or ignorance.
